question: Define finite graph and infinite graph with one example each.
expectedPoints: Finite graph: Contains finite number of vertices and edges (e.g., K4 with 4 vertices and 6 edges)
Infinite graph: Contains infinite number of vertices or edges (e.g., infinite path extending endlessly)

question: What is a bipartite graph? Explain with a proper example and show how it can be 2-colored.
expectedPoints: Definition: A graph whose vertices can be partitioned into two disjoint sets U and V such that every edge connects a vertex in U to a vertex in V
Example: K3,3 or a square cycle
2-coloring: Assign one color to all vertices in U and another color to all vertices in V

question: Define complete bipartite graph K(m,n). Find the number of edges in K4,5.
expectedPoints: Complete bipartite graph K(m,n) has m vertices in one set, n vertices in the other, with every vertex in first set connected to every vertex in second set
For K4,5: edges = m × n = 4 × 5 = 20 edges

question: State and explain the necessary and sufficient condition for a graph to be bipartite.
expectedPoints: A graph is bipartite if and only if it contains no odd cycle
Explanation: If a graph can be 2-colored (no adjacent vertices share color), it has no odd cycle; conversely, if it has no odd cycle, we can 2-color it
This is a fundamental theorem in graph theory

question: What is a star graph? Is it bipartite? Justify your answer.
expectedPoints: Star graph K1,n has one vertex in one partition and n vertices in the other partition
Yes, it is bipartite because it is a complete bipartite graph K1,n
It can be 2-colored: center gets color A, all outer vertices get color B

question: Differentiate between K3,3 and K3. Is K3,3 bipartite?
expectedPoints: K3 is complete graph with 3 vertices, all connected to each other (has triangle = odd cycle, NOT bipartite)
K3,3 is complete bipartite with partitions of size 3 and 3
K3,3 IS bipartite: vertices in one partition connect only to vertices in the other partition
K3 is NOT bipartite because it contains a 3-cycle (odd cycle)

question: Explain why C5 (cycle with 5 vertices) is not bipartite, while C4 is bipartite.
expectedPoints: C5 has odd number (5) of vertices in its cycle, forming an odd cycle (length 5)
A graph with an odd cycle cannot be bipartite (theorem)
C4 has 4 vertices in cycle, forming an even cycle (length 4)
C4 can be properly 2-colored: alternate colors around the 4-cycle

question: What is the significance of bipartite graphs in computer science applications? Discuss with examples of job assignment problem and marriage problem.
expectedPoints: Job Assignment Problem: Given n jobs and n workers, assign each job to exactly one worker - modeled as bipartite graph with workers and jobs as two partitions, edge exists if worker can do the job; finding maximum matching assigns maximum workers to jobs
Marriage Problem (Stable Marriage): Given set of men and women, find stable matching - bipartite graph where edges represent compatibility
Network Flow: Bipartite matching reduces to max-flow min-cut problem
Database Design: Entity-relationship modeling often uses bipartite concepts
Resource Allocation: CPU to tasks, memory to processes

question: Define and explain the following: (i) Finite graph (ii) Infinite graph (iii) Bipartite graph (iv) Complete bipartite graph K(m,n) (v) Star graph. For each type, give one practical application.
expectedPoints: (i) Finite graph: Limited vertices/edges; used in computer networks, social networks
(ii) Infinite graph: Unlimited vertices/edges; theoretical computer science, infinite data streams
(iii) Bipartite graph: Two-partition graph; scheduling, assignment problems
(iv) Complete bipartite K(m,n): All cross-connections; round-robin tournaments
(v) Star graph K1,n: Central hub with n nodes; hub-and-spoke network topology

question: Prove that a graph is bipartite if and only if it contains no odd cycle.
expectedPoints: (⇒) If graph is bipartite, then by definition vertices can be partitioned into U and V with edges only U→V; any cycle must alternate between U and V, thus must have even length (even number of edges)
(⇐) If graph has no odd cycle, we can 2-color it: pick any vertex, color it Red; all adjacent Blue; continue BFS coloring; no conflict will occur because if there were conflict, we'd have odd cycle
Alternative proof: By induction on number of vertices
This is a fundamental theorem (König's theorem) connecting bipartiteness to odd cycles

question: Explain the process of testing whether a given graph is bipartite. Apply this method to determine if the graph formed by a 6-cycle is bipartite.
expectedPoints: Testing method: Use BFS/DFS for 2-coloring
Algorithm: Start with any vertex, assign color 1; for each uncolored neighbor, assign opposite color; if we find two adjacent vertices with same color, NOT bipartite
For C6 (6-cycle): Start vertex A with color 1
Proceed BFS: neighbors of A get color 2, their neighbors get color 1
After 6 vertices, we return to A with alternating colors: all adjacent pairs have different colors
Conclusion: C6 is bipartite (all even cycles are bipartite)
General rule: C_n is bipartite iff n is even
